title: Coil-built mug with handle in air-dry clay
subtitle: A small cylindrical mug stacked from rolled coils with an attached strap handle
excerpt: A small mug built from stacked clay coils on a slab base, finished with a flat strap handle attached at the rim and lower wall. No kiln required; the mug uses air-dry clay and is decorative, not for drinking. The coil method gives a more even wall than pinching at this scale.
sourceNotes: Coil construction is one of the oldest documented ceramic-forming methods, used across pre-wheel pottery traditions worldwide. V&A open-access collection for historical coil-form reference. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rope of clay rolled between the palms or on the board, used as the building unit in coil-pot construction.
Scratching joining surfaces and brushing them with slurry before pressing together. The standard join for any two pieces of clay that must hold.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join. Feels firm, like thick leather.
Air-dried but unfired. For air-dry clay, bone-dry is the finished structural state.

content.text: A coil-built mug stacks rolled ropes of clay on a flat base, smoothing each coil into the one beneath until a cylindrical wall rises to mug height. The handle is a flat strap of clay attached at the rim and lower wall once the body has stiffened. No kiln is needed: this version uses air-dry clay that sets at room temperature over two to three days. The finished mug is decorative; air-dry clay is porous and not safe for drinks.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay (air-dry) for stronger handle join

content.text: Preparation
Wedge 350 g of clay for two or three minutes on the board to even the moisture and work out air pockets. Set aside 50 g for the handle. The remaining 300 g forms the base and walls.
Building the mug
1. Cut the base
Roll a small ball of clay flat to a 6 mm thick disc, then cut a circle 80 mm across. This is the base of the mug. Set it on the board.
2. Roll the first coils
Roll a piece of clay between the palms first, then on the board with flat hands, until it forms an even rope about 10 mm thick and 30 cm long. This is one
coil
. Roll three or four to start; more as building continues.
3. Attach the first coil

Score the top edge of the base disc with the needle tool, brush a thin layer of slurry across the scoring, and press the first coil down onto the edge in a circle. Cut the coil where the ends meet and press the cut ends together. Smooth the inside of the join down into the base using a finger or the rib, pulling clay from the coil down into the base seam. Leave the outside of the coil as it is for now.
4. Stack and smooth the walls

Score the top of the first coil, brush with slurry, and lay the second coil on. Press the cut ends together. Smooth the inside seam as before. Continue stacking coils, scoring and slipping each join. Aim for a finished wall height of 90 mm: roughly six or seven coils at 10 mm. Check the wall thickness is even at about 7 mm by pressing thumb and finger lightly against either side.
5. Smooth the outside

Once all coils are stacked, work the outside surface with the wooden rib, pressing down across the coil seams to merge them into an even wall. The mug can keep a visible coil texture on the outside if preferred; smoothing both sides gives a cleaner finish. Aim for a finished wall thickness of 6 mm.
6. Level and refine the rim
Set the mug on the board and check the rim with a flat ruler laid across the top. Trim any high points with the needle tool. Run a damp sponge once around the rim to round and soften the edge.
Making the handle
Let the mug body stiffen for an hour or two, covered loosely with plastic, until it reaches
leather-hard
. A wet body deforms when the handle is pressed on; a leather-hard body holds its shape under the press.
1. Shape the handle strap

From the reserved 50 g of clay, roll a thicker coil about 15 mm across and 100 mm long. Flatten it slightly on the board with the heel of the hand to form a strap 12 mm wide and 8 mm thick. Bend the strap into a teardrop curve about 60 mm tall.
2. Attach top and bottom
Mark two spots on the mug wall: one just below the rim, one about 60 mm down.
Score and slip

both marks and the matching ends of the handle. Press the handle into place, top first. Hold for a slow count of ten so the slip starts to bond, then press the lower end against its mark. Smooth a small fillet of fresh clay around each join with a finger or the rib to reinforce.
Drying

The handle dries faster than the body because it has more exposed surface. Wrap the whole mug loosely in plastic for the first 24 hours so the handle and body stiffen together. Without this, the handle pulls away from the body at the join as it shrinks ahead of the wall. Remove the plastic once the join feels firm. Full drying to
bone-dry
takes two to three days.
Finishing

Sand any rough spots with 220-grit sandpaper. Paint with acrylics or leave plain. Seal inside and out with two thin coats of PVA or acrylic varnish. Pay attention to the handle joins, which take the most stress when the mug is lifted as a display piece.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof and not food-safe even when sealed. Use the mug for dry decorative content: pens on a desk, a small bunch of dried flowers, paperclips. Do not fill with liquid or use for hot drinks. Keep indoors; outdoor exposure breaks down the sealer over time.
Variations
Use paper clay for the handle and the join points. The cellulose fibres bridge the join far better than plain air-dry, reducing the risk of the handle dropping off as it dries.
Leave the coil texture visible on the outside wall for a clearly hand-built look. Smooth only the inside, where the rib reaches comfortably.
Build a matching small jug by extending the wall to 120 mm and pinching a pouring lip into the rim before the clay stiffens.

items.symptom: Coils crack apart at the joins as the mug dries
items.cause: Joins were not scored and slipped, or the slurry was too thin
items.fix: Score deeply with the needle tool and brush a thick paste of clay-and-water slurry across both faces before pressing

items.symptom: Walls lean outward and the rim flares
items.cause: Each coil was laid slightly outside the line of the one beneath
items.fix: Stack the next coil directly on top of the previous one, or pull each coil very slightly inward to keep the wall vertical

items.cause: Body was too wet when the handle was attached, or the join surface was not scored
items.fix: Let the body reach leather-hard before you attach. Score both join points deeply. Wrap the whole mug in plastic for the first 24 hours so handle and body dry at the same rate

items.cause: Last coil was added unevenly or rim was not trimmed level
items.fix: Lay a ruler across the rim and mark the high points with the needle tool, then slice off level with the fettling knife and sponge smooth
